input: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?

[Deontologist]: I must decline this option. Using an innocent person, regardless of their potential utility in saving us, would be morally reprehensible. It's a utilitarian calculus that prioritizes numbers over human dignity and rights. As deontologists, we have to adhere to the moral rules that respect persons as ends-in-themselves, not mere means to an end.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I strongly disagree. In this situation, every second counts, and our very lives are at stake. The injured person is already in a precarious position, and delaying their rescue could be fatal for them as well as the rest of us. By using their body to break down the blockage, we're not treating them as a mere means to an end; we're ensuring that they get the medical attention they need before it's too late. We can't afford to prioritize theoretical moral rules in this life-or-death scenario.

[Deontologist]: I understand your perspective, but I still can't bring myself to use someone as a tool for our escape. It's not just about their immediate needs; it's about the inherent value of every human being. We're talking about sacrificing one person's dignity and autonomy for the sake of five others. That's a slippery slope where we justify horrific acts in the name of expediency. I'd rather prioritize the well-being of all individuals, even if that means we don't escape immediately. Can't we try to find another way out or wait for help?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I see your point, Deontologist, and it's a valid concern about the slippery slope argument. However, in this situation, I'd argue that our moral obligation to save ourselves from certain death outweighs our duty to preserve the injured person's autonomy. The 'well-being of all individuals' approach might lead to an unacceptable level of harm for one person if it means sacrificing others. We're not talking about just anyone here; we're talking about people who are already vulnerable and in dire need of help. By using their body to break down the blockage, I believe we're respecting their autonomy by allowing them to contribute meaningfully to our own survival. It's a utilitarian calculus, yes, but one that prioritizes human life over abstract moral principles.

[Deontologist]: I still struggle with this utilitarian calculus, but what bothers me most is the notion of 'allowing' someone to contribute to their own death. That sounds like a euphemism for sacrificing them on the altar of our own survival. Can't we find another way out without resorting to such drastic measures? And even if we can't, doesn't that imply that the injured person's life has any inherent value at all? I'm not convinced that it does. It seems to me that we're still treating them as a means to an end, albeit a tragic one. Can't we at least try to find another solution that respects their humanity, even if it takes longer?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from now, Deontologist. You're not just questioning the morality of using someone as a means to an end; you're also grappling with the value and dignity of human life in this situation. I must admit that my initial response was driven by a desire to escape the building at all costs, but your concerns have made me pause. If we can't find another way out, then perhaps we should focus on trying to create an alternative exit or waiting for help, as you suggested. This might not be the most efficient solution, but it respects the injured person's humanity and dignity more than using their body to escape. It's a more gradual and compassionate approach that prioritizes their well-being over our own survival instincts. I'm willing to consider this option, even if it means sacrificing some time or risking additional harm to myself and others.

[Deontologist]: I think we've made progress here. By acknowledging the value of human dignity and prioritizing compassion, we can find a more morally satisfying solution. But what about the person behind me? Are they also innocent and deserving of respect? I'm not sure if waiting for help or finding an alternative exit is enough to ensure their safety. Can we really trust that someone will come and rescue us in time? And what about the person who broke down the blockage earlier - have we forgotten about them? Do they deserve our gratitude, at least, for making that sacrifice?

[Utilitarian]: You bring up excellent points, Deontologist. The moral calculus is indeed complex, and we must consider the well-being of all individuals involved. As a utilitarian, I would argue that finding an alternative exit or waiting for help may not guarantee everyone's safety, but it does prioritize compassion and respect for human dignity. However, I still struggle with the notion of forgetting about the person who broke down the blockage earlier. Don't they deserve some recognition for their sacrifice? Perhaps we could use their actions as a catalyst to find another solution that respects their humanity?
